mnemonics.title: LIFO - Last In First Out
mnemonics.content: LIFO = Last In First Out — The last element added to the stack is the first one to be removed. Remember: Like a stack of plates at a buffet — you take the top plate first (the last one placed).

mnemonics.title: PUSH POP PEEK
mnemonics.content: PUSH = Put item on top, POP = Take item from top, PEEK = Look at top without removing. Think: Push it up, Pop it off, Peek at it.

mnemonics.title: Overflow/Underflow Rule
mnemonics.content: Overflow = Stack is full, cannot push (too much). Underflow = Stack is empty, cannot pop (too little). Remember: Overflow when overflowing with items, Underflow when under (empty).

mnemonics.title: Array vs Linked List
mnemonics.content: Array Stack = Fixed size, contiguous memory. Linked List Stack = Dynamic size, scattered memory. Remember: Array = A for Always fixed, Linked List = L for Lasting (grows as needed).

mnemonics.title: Top Pointer Magic
mnemonics.content: Top pointer in array implementation starts at -1 (empty) and increments. In linked list, top is head pointer. Remember: -1 means nothing there, 0 means first position filled.

acronyms.usage: The fundamental principle of stack operation — use when describing how elements are added and removed
Stack is an ADT that defines operations without implementation details — use when discussing theoretical concept
Push, Pop, and Peek operations take constant time — use when analyzing time complexity
Note: Queue uses FIFO, Stack uses LIFO — use in comparisons with other data structures
Points to the most recently added element — use when discussing stack tracking mechanism

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a vertical box with compartments numbered from bottom (0) to top. The TOP pointer is like an arrow pointing to the highest occupied compartment. When you PUSH, the arrow moves up one level. When you POP, it moves down.

Think of a chain hanging from a hook. Each link is a node containing data and a pointer to the next link. The hook (TOP) holds the first link. Adding a node puts a new link on the hook; removing takes the hook's link.
Picture a cup filled to the brim. Overflow happens when you try to add more water — it spills. In array-based stack, the 'cup' (array) has a fixed capacity.
Imagine an empty plate rack. Underflow occurs when you try to take a plate but none exist — the rack is bare. This happens when TOP = -1 (array) or TOP = NULL (linked list).
Imagine looking at the top item of a stack without touching it — like reading the title of the top book in a pile without moving the pile.

analogies.analogy: At a wedding buffet, clean plates are stacked. You always take the top plate (last placed), and when a new batch comes, they're placed on top. This is PUSH (add to top) and POP (remove from top) — Last In First Out!

Every webpage you visit gets pushed onto a stack. When you click 'back', you POP the previous page. The most recent page is always at the top and accessed first — exactly like a stack's LIFO behavior.
In a text editor, every action is pushed onto an undo stack. To undo, you POP the last action. The 'Undo' button always reverses your most recent action first — classic stack application.

When a function calls another function, the first function's execution is paused and pushed onto a stack. The new function runs, then pops the previous one to resume. This is how function calls work internally!

Think of Indian practice of stacking documents or 'pratilipi' — you add new papers on top, and when you need to find something, you start from the top. You cannot access middle papers without removing top ones first.
When packing a suitcase, you place clothes in layers — last item placed (on top) is first to be removed when unpacking. This everyday activity demonstrates LIFO perfectly.
